Title: Quantitative trait loci and differential gene expression analyses reveal the genetic basis for negatively associated β-carotene and starch content in hexaploid sweetpotato [Ipomoea batatas (L.) Lam.]

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: major calorie reserve and dry matter component
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher SuSY expression is associated with higher starch content; reduced SuSY expression in orange-fleshed material is associated with reduced starch and inverse association with β-carotene.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed similarly in 10 and 20 DAT roots of Beauregard and Tanzania; remains elevated through fibrous and storage roots in Tanzania; declines in Beauregard from 30 DAT and is undetectable in 50 DAT storage roots.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: QTL co-localization; physical linkage analysis; RNA-seq differential expression between Beauregard and Tanzania roots
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SuSY (itf03g05100) lies within the major LG3 QTL affecting starch, dry matter, β-carotene, and flesh color. 2. SuSY is physically linked to PSY within 12.2 kb with no intervening genes. 3. SuSY is downregulated in Beauregard relative to Tanzania in 30 DAT storage roots (log2FC = -2.59; adj. p = 1.8E-12), consistent with lower starch and higher β-carotene in orange-fleshed genotypes.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field phenotyping across Peru environments including terminal drought and control, plus optimal humid tropic environment; gene expression measured during root development under parallel growth conditions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as Common Gene because it is a key starch-biosynthesis-associated metabolic gene discussed as supplying ADP-glucose precursor in sink tissue, but not the principal carotenoid pathway enzyme/regulator focus. The paper hypothesizes inhibition of SuSY activity during chromoplast biogenesis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Association with increased starch opposes β-carotene accumulation due to negative correlation between the traits.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful haplotype target for breaking unfavorable linkage with PSY or selecting favorable starch/β-carotene balance in breeding; candidate for reverse-genetic validation and potential editing to alter starch-carotenoid balance.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: GBSpoly genotyping; RNA-seq expression profiling of developing roots

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms pathway entry metabolite leading to β-carotene
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: storage roots; also fibrous roots in Beauregard during development
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes β-carotene biosynthesis and accumulation; favorable PSY haplotype is associated with orange flesh and reduced starch through linked effects with SuSY.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in all sampled Beauregard roots with peak at 40 DAT storage roots; expressed in Tanzania roots but absent in 30 DAT storage roots and very low at 50 DAT fibrous and storage roots; differentially expressed in 50 DAT storage roots (Beauregard vs Tanzania, log2FC = 2.34, adj. p = 4.2E-6).
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Major QTL co-localization; positional candidate within LG3 peak; RNA-seq differential expression; prior association mapping cited by authors
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PSY (itf03g05110) is located within the major LG3 QTL controlling β-carotene, flesh color, starch, and dry matter. 2. PSY is close to the shared QTL peaks and is differentially expressed between orange-fleshed Beauregard and white-fleshed Tanzania in 50 DAT storage roots. 3. PSY is physically linked to SuSY, supporting a genetic basis for the negative β-carotene–starch association.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: OR/IbOr; SuSY (physical linkage, not biochemical interaction directly proven in this paper)
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: GBSpoly genotyping; RNA-seq expression profiling of developing roots
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Favorable β-carotene haplotype is associated with reduced starch/dry matter due to linked QTL effects.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper interprets PSY as cis-acting major locus on LG3 and part of interaction with IbOr on LG12. Expression alone is not sufficient to explain carotenoid accumulation because both parents express PSY.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Top candidate for functional markers, haplotype mining, and gene editing to improve provitamin A; breeding should consider tight linkage with SuSY and combine with favorable IbOr haplotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Root developmental stages 10–50 DAT; multi-environment phenotyping across Peru and Uganda contexts

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Downregulated between Beauregard and Tanzania in 30 and 50 DAT storage roots according to the paper.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Positional candidate under QTL; RNA-seq differential expression; prior association with flesh color cited by authors
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCYB (itf04g32080) underlies a carotenoid-related QTL linked to flesh color. 2. It had been previously associated with flesh color in a panel of orange- and white-fleshed sweetpotato parents. 3. The gene is differentially expressed between Beauregard and Tanzania in 30 and 50 DAT storage roots.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as additional carotenoid biosynthetic candidate rather than the main driver of the trait relationship; included because it is explicitly highlighted in Results as a candidate under carotenoid-related QTL.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary candidate for improving carotenoid composition/flesh color, likely best used in combination with PSY and IbOr haplotypes.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Identified as an additional carotenoid biosynthetic gene underlying carotenoid-related QTL.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDS (itf03g06560) lies under carotenoid-related QTL for β-carotene/flesh color. 2. It is considered an additional candidate contributing to carotenoid pathway variation. 3. It was not presented as the primary causal gene compared with PSY and Or.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Background/secondary candidate gene within QTL region; evidence weaker than for PSY and IbOr.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential supplementary target in carotenoid engineering after validating causal haplotypes.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts violaxanthin to antheraxanthin in the xanthophyll cycle.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: downstream metabolism/degradation-related branch of β-carotene-derived carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Highlighted as an additional carotenoid metabolism gene under carotenoid-related QTL.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VDE (itf03g11440) underlies QTL for β-carotene/flesh color. 2. It functions downstream of β-carotene in carotenoid metabolism. 3. The paper treats it as a secondary candidate relative to PSY and Or.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence is positional rather than direct functional validation in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible modifier target for carotenoid composition after primary loci are optimized.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes chromoplast formation and β-carotene accumulation while contributing to the negative association with starch.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in both Beauregard and Tanzania roots throughout the developmental time course; not differentially expressed under the study cutoff.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Major QTL co-localization on LG12; positional candidate near peak marker; RNA-seq expression profiling; prior functional evidence in sweetpotato and other species cited by authors
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: post-transcriptional regulation of PSY protein level and enzyme activity; promotes amyloplast-to-chromoplast transition
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Enhances PSY function/activity and carotenoid accumulation; acts pleiotropically with PSY
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Molecular switch for chromoplast biogenesis and major trans-acting determinant of carotenoid accumulation at the LG12 QTL
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with PSY protein as reported in literature cited by the paper
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: developmental root differentiation; sequence haplotype variation inferred
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Haplotypes increasing β-carotene are associated with reduced starch through coordinated chromoplast biogenesis.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Or (itf12g24270) lies near the major LG12 QTL peak affecting β-carotene, starch, dry matter, and flesh color. 2. It is highly expressed in both parents during root development but is not differentially expressed, implying sequence/haplotype rather than transcript abundance may underlie the phenotype. 3. The study concludes that IbOr interacts with PSY in trans to promote chromoplast biogenesis and β-carotene accumulation, contributing to reduced starch.
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: GBSpoly genotyping; RNA-seq of developing roots
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Root developmental stages 10–50 DAT; multi-environment field phenotyping for trait mapping
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although not differentially expressed, Or is treated by the authors as one of the two principal candidate genes explaining the trait relationship. They discuss possible causative sequence variants analogous to the 'golden SNP' known in other species.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime target for haplotype-assisted breeding, marker development, and possible editing; best deployed with favorable PSY haplotypes because expression alone does not guarantee carotenoid accumulation.
